lis-1.question: Complete: Concern about delayed ___ administration.
lis-1.script: So, during the handover I noticed the six o'clock antibiotic dose for Mr Patel went in at nearly eight. That delay is a real concern. Let's document it properly and make sure the next dose is on time.

lis-2.question: Complete: Patient was ___ to ED after a fall.
lis-2.script: The patient had a fall at home and was referred to the emergency department. Pretty straightforward — just make sure you spell referred correctly.

lis-3.question: Complete: Staff need ___ time for training.
lis-3.script: Mandatory training? It's valuable, no question. But finding protected time during a busy shift? That's the real challenge. We support it but the practical side is tough.

lis-4.question: Complete: Patient at high risk of ___ if unattended.
lis-4.script: Look, Mrs Green is unsteady on her feet. If she stands up unattended, she's at high risk of falling. So one staff member needs to stay with her at all times.

lis-5.question: Complete: Medication increased: ___.
lis-5.script: The clinician reviewed his diabetes today and increased the metformin. Keep an eye on that spelling in your notes.

lis-6.question: Complete: Patient may need ___ if breathless.
lis-6.script: Here's what I'm worried about: if Mr Lewis becomes breathless, we need to check his oxygen saturation right away. He may need supplemental oxygen while we contact the team.

lis-7.question: Complete: Telehealth works best with periodic ___ review.
lis-7.script: Dr Okoye made a great point about telehealth. It works well for check-ins, but you've got to combine it with periodic in-person review. Remote care shouldn't replace face-to-face completely.

lis-8.question: Complete: Reduce unnecessary ___ prescribing.
lis-8.script: The main message from today's presentation is clear: we need to reduce unnecessary broad-spectrum prescribing. Choose the narrowest effective option and review when culture results come back.

lis-9.question: Complete: Recurrence after ___ months clear.
lis-9.script: Ray Sands came in today. He said the symptoms came back after he'd been clear for six months. So we're looking at a recurrence here.

lis-10.question: Complete: Store unopened insulin in ___.
lis-10.script: For insulin storage: keep unopened pens in the refrigerator. Once opened, just follow the label instructions and write the date on it.

lis-11.question: Complete: Check ___ saturation and notify doctor.
lis-11.script: Post-surgery and suddenly breathless? That's our cue to check oxygen saturation immediately and notify the doctor. Don't wait on this one.

lis-12.question: Complete: Programme targets ___ brain injury.
lis-12.script: This weight management programme — it's designed specifically for adults with acquired brain injury. The rehab side of things makes healthy routines harder for them.

lis-13.question: Complete: Treatment mentioned: ___ therapy.
lis-13.script: In the rheumatology meeting, the team discussed next steps. They're thinking about shockwave therapy as the next option to try.

lis-14.question: Complete: New policy reduces ___ appointments.
lis-14.script: Alright team, I'm rolling out a new reminder policy. We'll send patients a message two days before their booking, and they can cancel by text. Our goal: reduce missed appointments.

lis-15.question: Complete: Patient ___ chest pain since morning.
lis-15.script: This patient has had chest pain since morning. It's ongoing, so we need to act on it.

lis-16.question: Complete: Social worker recommends ___ visit.
lis-16.script: Before we discharge her this weekend, the social worker recommends a home visit. We need to check access, mobility, and what support she's got in place.

lis-17.question: Complete: Patient reports ___ in both knees.
lis-17.script: The patient came in complaining of aches in both knees, especially after stairs. That's the main symptom we're working with.

lis-18.question: Complete: Give antibiotics within ___ of recognition.
lis-18.script: Once sepsis is recognised, time is critical. Antibiotics should be given within one hour of recognition. Don't wait for the next ward round.

lis-19.question: Complete: Doctor asked to clarify whether ___ is required.
lis-19.script: The imaging request came through but it doesn't say whether contrast is needed. Can the requesting doctor clarify that before we proceed?

lis-20.question: Complete: Date of birth: 14 ___ 1968.
lis-20.script: Date of birth: the fourteenth of March, nineteen sixty-eight.

lis-21.question: Complete: Chart shows duplicate ___.
lis-21.script: Looking at this chart, I see a duplicate antihypertensive. Same class of blood pressure med listed twice. We need to fix this before the next dose goes out.

lis-22.question: Complete: Biggest modifiable risk is ___.
lis-22.script: When we talk about modifiable fall risks, polypharmacy is the big one — especially sedating drugs. A structured medication review can prevent a lot of falls.
lis-22.speechScript: Among the risks we can change, the largest is poly pharmacy, particularly the use of sedating drugs. A structured medication review can therefore prevent many falls.

lis-23.question: Complete: Prescribed dose: ___ mg twice daily.
lis-23.script: The dose is five hundred milligrams twice daily. Write that down exactly.

lis-24.question: Complete: Consultant ___ to suggestion.
lis-24.script: The nurse made a good suggestion during the MDT. The consultant agreed with it and asked for a written plan so the whole team can follow it.

lis-25.question: Complete: Programme prioritises ___ neuropathy.
lis-25.script: Our foot screening programme prioritises patients with peripheral neuropathy. Why? Because reduced sensation means they might not notice an injury forming.

lis-26.question: Complete: Chest pain suggests ___ cause.
lis-26.script: You know what? This chest pain isn't your typical angina. It's sharp and changes with position. That points me toward oesophageal spasm or costochondritis rather than the heart. We'll do a stress echo to be safe, but I think we're looking at something non-cardiac here.

lis-27.question: Complete: Patient advised to avoid ___ before the stress test.
lis-27.script: Important for the stress test: no caffeine for twenty-four hours beforehand. That means coffee, tea, cola, chocolate — none of it.

lis-28.question: Complete: Clarithromycin may elevate ___ levels.
lis-28.script: Right, so here's the issue. This patient's on warfarin, and now we've added clarithromycin. That's a significant interaction — the clarithromycin inhibits the enzymes that break down warfarin, so the INR could climb dangerously high. I'd reduce the warfarin dose and recheck in forty-eight hours.

lis-29.question: Complete: Speaker is ___ supportive of new protocol.
lis-29.script: The new protocol? It's evidence-based and I support the idea. But let's be real — the paperwork is heavy and in a busy ED that could slow things down instead of helping. So I'm cautiously supportive, but the implementation needs work.

lis-30.question: Complete: Patient diagnosed with ___.
lis-30.script: The ultrasound result is back. It shows acute cholecystitis with gallstones. So we're referring for a laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

lis-31.question: Complete: Nurse concerned about ___ and amiodarone.
lis-31.script: I'm looking at the transfer summary and something doesn't add up. The patient was on apixaban for AF and amiodarone for rhythm control. But the GP letter only mentions apixaban. Was the amiodarone stopped, or just left off the form? I need to find out.

lis-32.question: Complete: Highest nephropathy risk with eGFR below ___.
lis-32.script: Contrast-induced nephropathy isn't common, but the risk goes way up when the eGFR is below 30 and they're on metformin. In that scenario we stop metformin for two days and hydrate well before the scan.

lis-33.question: Complete: Apply ___ cream twice daily.
lis-33.script: The dermatologist has prescribed one percent hydrocortisone cream. Apply it to the affected area twice daily for seven days. Not a day longer without review.

lis-34.question: Complete: Discharge summary needs clear ___ plan.
lis-34.script: The discharge summary is thorough, I'll give it that. But here's the problem — there's no follow-up plan with specific timeframes. When should the GP see them? When are the bloods due? Without that information, the GP is flying blind.

lis-35.question: Complete: Student misses ___ presentation.
lis-35.script: The medical student picked up the classic heart failure signs — good start. But here's what they missed: this patient's symptoms came on over six months, not weeks. That slow, indolent course points to constrictive pericarditis, not systolic failure. So they're partially right, but the timing changes everything.

lis-36.question: Complete: Patient to receive ___ via PEG tube.
lis-36.script: We need to start nasogastric tube feeding. Pass an NGT and confirm position with a chest X-ray before commencing the feed.

lis-37.question: Complete: Non-___ therapy should precede opioids.
lis-37.script: Before you write that opioid script, think about this. Physiotherapy, CBT, and activity changes — those have Level 1 evidence for chronic back pain. Opioids have their place, but only after non-pharmacological options have been properly tried. Don't skip the basics.

lis-38.question: Complete: Escalate if NEWS score above ___.
lis-38.script: Use the NEWS2 chart. If the total is five or more — or any single parameter hits three — call the registrar straight away. Don't wait for the next set of observations.

lis-39.question: Complete: Patient allergic to ___.
lis-39.script: Important: this patient has a documented allergy to penicillamine. Make sure it's flagged on the drug chart and avoid any medications containing it.

lis-40.question: Complete: Study lacks ___ introducing bias.
lis-40.script: The results for this new blood thinner look good, I won't deny it. But the study was open-label — no blinding at all. And that introduces performance bias. A double-blind randomised trial would've been far more convincing.

lis-41.question: Complete: CT head showed no acute ___.
lis-41.script: We reviewed the CT head with radiology. The good news: no acute intracranial haemorrhage. So no neurosurgery needed at this point.

lis-42.question: Complete: Switch antiemetic to ___.
lis-42.script: The ondansetron isn't touching her nausea, so let's switch things up. I want to try cyclizine instead — it works through a different pathway and might work better for this type of post-op nausea.

lis-43.question: Complete: Blood culture grew ___.
lis-43.script: The blood culture came back positive for Staphylococcus aureus, and it's sensitive to flucloxacillin. So we'll start IV flucloxacillin and arrange an echo to check for endocarditis.

lis-44.question: Complete: Mobilise within ___ hours of surgery.
lis-44.script: We need to get her moving within twenty-four hours of surgery, assuming she's haemodynamically stable and not bleeding. Early mobilisation cuts the risk of DVT and chest infection.

lis-45.question: Complete: Discharge includes ___ units of insulin.
lis-45.script: Her discharge insulin is sorted: thirty-four units of Lantus at night, plus NovoRapid with meals according to the sliding scale.

lis-46.question: Complete: Speech pathologist recommends ___ diet.
lis-46.script: Following the swallow assessment, she's at risk of aspiration with thin fluids. So I'm recommending a soft diet with Level 2 thickened fluids. We'll reassess in three days.

lis-47.question: Complete: Suspected ___ arteritis.
lis-47.script: A sixty-five-year-old with sudden vision loss, headache, and jaw claudication — that's giant cell arteritis until proven otherwise. Start high-dose steroids now and arrange a temporal artery biopsy.

lis-48.question: Complete: Biopsy shows ___ carcinoma.
lis-48.script: The breast biopsy confirms invasive ductal carcinoma. It's oestrogen receptor positive and HER2 negative. We'll discuss surgical options and consider endocrine therapy.

lis-49.question: Complete: Start ___ at low dose with gradual titration.
lis-49.script: She's never tried an SSRI before, and given her panic disorder history, I'd start sertraline at twenty-five milligrams daily. Low and slow — that way we minimise the initial anxiety side effect.

lis-50.question: Complete: Apply ___ oral suspension four times daily.
lis-50.script: For the oral thrush, prescribe nystatin oral suspension. Four times a day after meals, and ask the patient to hold it in their mouth for as long as possible before swallowing.

lis-51.question: Complete: Recommend induction at ___ weeks.
lis-51.script: With the gestational diabetes and the baby measuring large for dates, I'm recommending induction at thirty-eight weeks. Going beyond that increases the risk of shoulder dystocia.

lis-52.question: Complete: IV access via ___ line.
lis-52.script: We've secured peripheral IV access in the left forearm. The line's running well and we've started normal saline at a hundred mils per hour.

lis-53.question: Complete: Rash likely due to ___ reaction.
lis-53.script: The rash started about ten days after she began lisinopril. That timing is pretty classic for a drug eruption. Let's stop the lisinopril and switch to an ARB instead.

lis-54.question: Complete: MRI shows ___ at L4-L5.
lis-54.script: The lumbar spine MRI confirms a disc prolapse at L4-L5 with nerve root impingement. We'll try conservative management for six weeks before discussing surgery.

lis-55.question: Complete: Anaesthetist concerned about difficult ___.
lis-55.script: On examination, the patient's mouth opening is less than three centimetres with a Mallampati score of four. That signals a potentially difficult airway. I'd prefer an awake fibreoptic intubation.

lis-56.question: Complete: Antibiotic changed to ___.
lis-56.script: Based on the allergy history and sputum culture, we're switching from co-amoxiclav to doxycycline. Seven-day course.

lis-57.question: Complete: Fever likely due to ___ illness.
lis-57.script: The rash and fever pattern point to a viral exanthem. Antibiotics won't help here — this should resolve on its own in a few days. Manage the fever with paracetamol and ibuprofen.

lis-58.question: Complete: Fracture type: ___ of distal radius.
lis-58.script: The X-ray shows a Colles fracture of the distal radius with dorsal angulation. We'll reduce it in ED and apply a back slab.

lis-59.question: Complete: Reduce ___ intake to below 2 grams daily.
lis-59.script: For hypertension, the single most effective dietary change is reducing sodium to below two grams a day. That means cutting processed foods and not adding salt at the table.

lis-60.question: Complete: ECG shows ___ fibrillation.
lis-60.script: The ECG shows atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response around a hundred and forty. We'll rate-control with bisoprolol and arrange a TOE before cardioversion.

lis-61.question: Complete: Patient had transient ___ attack.
lis-61.script: The symptoms lasted forty minutes and resolved completely. That's a transient ischaemic attack. We need an urgent MRI brain, carotid doppler, and start dual antiplatelet therapy today.

lis-62.question: Complete: Palliative plan prioritises ___ control.
lis-62.script: The palliative care plan focuses on symptom control — managing pain, nausea, and breathlessness. We'll involve the community palliative team for support at home.

lis-63.question: Complete: Start ___ and refer for physiotherapy.
lis-63.script: With active synovitis in multiple joints and raised inflammatory markers, I'd start methotrexate at fifteen milligrams weekly. And I'll refer her for physiotherapy to maintain function.

lis-64.question: Complete: Fluid challenge of ___ mils given.
lis-64.script: We gave a five hundred mil fluid challenge of compound sodium lactate over fifteen minutes. The blood pressure came up from ninety systolic to a hundred and ten.

lis-65.question: Complete: Recommended approach is ___ chemotherapy.
lis-65.script: For this stage three rectal cancer, the recommended approach is neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy followed by surgical resection. That gives us the best shot at clear margins.

lis-66.question: Complete: Started on ___ for hyperkalaemia.
lis-66.script: The potassium was six point eight. We gave IV calcium gluconate for cardiac protection, then insulin and dextrose to shift the potassium back into the cells.

lis-67.question: Complete: Main challenge is ___ home placement.
lis-67.script: The patient's medically fit for discharge, but lives alone with no family around. Finding a nursing home placement at short notice is proving difficult. We might need a transitional care bed first.

lis-68.question: Complete: Urine dip shows ___ and blood.
lis-68.script: The urine dipstick is positive for protein and blood. With her history of hypertension and ankle swelling, we need to check renal function and arrange an ultrasound.

lis-69.question: Complete: Suspected primary ___.
lis-69.script: Look at this: hypertension, low potassium, and metabolic alkalosis. That triad raises the suspicion of primary hyperaldosteronism. Let's check the aldosterone-to-renin ratio and do a CT adrenal.

lis-70.question: Complete: Patient transferred to ___ for further management.
lis-70.script: The GCS is dropping and she needs ventilatory support, so we've transferred the patient to the intensive care unit for ongoing management.

lis-71.question: Complete: Sputum sample must be sent for ___ staining.
lis-71.script: Because the cough has lasted six weeks with night sweats and weight loss, send an early morning sputum sample for acid-fast bacilli staining and mycobacterial culture.

lis-72.question: Complete: eGFR is borderline for ___ administration.
lis-72.script: Her eGFR is twenty-nine, so gadolinium administration is borderline and must be discussed with radiology before the MRI slot is confirmed.

lis-73.question: Complete: Patient needs ___ precautions after varicella exposure.
lis-73.script: The patient is immunosuppressed and has had significant varicella exposure. Move him to a negative-pressure room and use airborne precautions until infection control reviews him.

lis-74.question: Complete: Consultant suspects ___ embolism.
lis-74.script: Given the pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, and recent long-haul flight, the consultant suspects pulmonary embolism and wants a D-dimer followed by CTPA if indicated.

lis-75.question: Complete: Repeat troponin at ___ hours.
lis-75.script: The initial troponin is negative but the pain started only ninety minutes ago. Repeat troponin at three hours before deciding whether he can go home.

lis-76.question: Complete: Withhold ___ before contrast scan.
lis-76.script: Because she is dehydrated and needs contrast imaging, withhold metformin on the day of the scan and restart only after renal function is rechecked.

lis-77.question: Complete: Start ___ for suspected adrenal crisis.
lis-77.script: Do not wait for the cortisol result. In suspected adrenal crisis, start intravenous hydrocortisone immediately with saline resuscitation.

lis-78.question: Complete: Audiology notes ___ hearing loss.
lis-78.script: The audiogram shows asymmetrical sensorineural hearing loss, so the ENT team wants an MRI internal auditory meatus to exclude an acoustic neuroma.

lis-79.question: Complete: Blood film shows ___ cells.
lis-79.script: The platelet count has crashed and the blood film shows schistocytes. That combination raises concern for microangiopathic haemolysis.

lis-80.question: Complete: Administer ___ before paracentesis.
lis-80.script: For large-volume paracentesis, administer albumin afterwards to reduce circulatory dysfunction. Please calculate the dose based on litres drained.

lis-81.question: Complete: Use ___ suture for facial laceration.
lis-81.script: For this child with a small facial laceration, use absorbable suture so we do not need a distressing removal appointment next week.

lis-82.question: Complete: Trial excluded patients with ___ insufficiency.
lis-82.script: The trial excluded patients with adrenal insufficiency, recent steroid use, or pituitary disease because those factors would distort cortisol-response outcomes.

lis-83.question: Complete: Monitor for ___ syndrome after SSRI change.
lis-83.script: After increasing the SSRI while he is still taking tramadol, monitor closely for serotonin syndrome: agitation, tremor, sweating, and diarrhoea.

lis-84.question: Complete: Patient needs ___ prophylaxis after needle-stick.
lis-84.script: This was a high-risk needle-stick injury from an unknown source. Start post-exposure prophylaxis today after baseline HIV, hepatitis B, and hepatitis C bloods.

lis-85.question: Complete: Ultrasound confirms ___ pregnancy.
lis-85.script: The ultrasound confirms an ectopic pregnancy in the right fallopian tube with free fluid in the pelvis. The gynaecology registrar is coming now.

lis-86.question: Complete: Swallow assessment showed silent ___.
lis-86.script: The video swallow assessment showed silent aspiration with thin fluids. Keep her nil by mouth until speech pathology reviews the thickened-fluid plan.

lis-87.question: Complete: Drug causing ototoxicity: ___.
lis-87.script: His hearing deteriorated after several high trough levels. Gentamicin is the likely ototoxic drug, so stop it and document the adverse reaction.

lis-88.question: Complete: Target oxygen saturation is ___ percent.
lis-88.script: Because of his chronic carbon dioxide retention, prescribe controlled oxygen and document the target as 88 to 92 percent, not the usual higher range.

lis-89.question: Complete: Treat suspected ___ fasciitis urgently.
lis-89.script: The pain is out of proportion and the skin is rapidly discolouring. Treat suspected necrotising fasciitis urgently and call surgery before imaging delays care.

lis-90.question: Complete: Reduce tacrolimus because of ___.
lis-90.script: The tacrolimus trough is high and creatinine has doubled. Reduce the dose because of nephrotoxicity and repeat levels in forty-eight hours.

lis-91.question: Complete: Plan ___ drainage for empyema.
lis-91.script: The CT chest shows a loculated pleural collection with gas bubbles. Respiratory wants image-guided intercostal drainage for empyema this afternoon.

lis-92.question: Complete: Symptoms suggest ___ vertigo.
lis-92.script: The vertigo lasts seconds and is triggered when she rolls over in bed. That pattern suggests benign paroxysmal positional vertigo rather than a stroke.

lis-93.question: Complete: Start ___ replacement after thyroidectomy.
lis-93.script: Following total thyroidectomy, start levothyroxine replacement and check calcium because hypoparathyroidism can present with tingling or cramps.

lis-94.question: Complete: Consultant mentions ___ anaemia.
lis-94.script: The bilirubin is unconjugated, LDH is high, and haptoglobin is low. The consultant mentions haemolytic anaemia and requests a direct antiglobulin test.

lis-95.question: Complete: Refer for ___ ultrasound.
lis-95.script: With postmenopausal bleeding, refer for transvaginal ultrasound to measure endometrial thickness before deciding whether biopsy is needed.

lis-96.question: Complete: Use ___ precautions for C. difficile.
lis-96.script: For suspected C. difficile diarrhoea, use contact precautions, isolate the patient, and clean surfaces with sporicidal disinfectant.

lis-97.question: Complete: Genetic test shows ___ mutation.
lis-97.script: Her genetic test shows a pathogenic BRCA1 mutation, so she needs counselling about breast screening, ovarian risk, and cascade testing for relatives.

lis-98.question: Complete: Patient is awaiting ___ biopsy.
lis-98.script: After the wide local excision, the patient is awaiting sentinel node biopsy to stage the melanoma before the oncology plan is finalised.

lis-99.question: Complete: Lecture criticizes lack of ___ concealment.
lis-99.script: The lecturer's main criticism is the lack of allocation concealment. Without it, recruiters may unconsciously influence which participants enter each treatment arm.

lis-100.question: Complete: Clinic advises ___ exercises for vestibular rehab.
lis-100.script: For vestibular rehabilitation, the clinic advises gaze-stabilisation exercises twice daily, especially while turning the head slowly from side to side.
